description: Loose granular material formed from broken rock, found on beaches and in deserts. 'Kirua' names the countless tiny fragments—what remains when stone surrenders to time and water, the soft accumulation of ages.
sound_symbolism: The word shifts and settles. 'Ki' strikes with countless fine grains, particles beyond number. 'Ru' shifts and runs, sand in motion. Then 'a' settles into temporary stillness, grains coming to rest.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun; a mass noun, usually uncountable. Takes the classifier 'themo' when counted as masses or areas. What remains when 'kerou' (stone) breaks down; gathers at 'leru' (beach). Associated with deserts, hourglasses, and impermanence.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Sand teaches impermanence through accumulation—each grain was once mountain, and time transforms all solidity to particles.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Sand marked time in hourglasses and provided material for glassmaking—the tiny grains serving human needs through transformation.
